Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.", "Figure 12.", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\mu \mathrm{m}$ ),  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in", "NMVOC emissions were speciated to SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms following the explicit species mapping approach documented in Li et al. (2014) (see Fig. 3). Finally, emissions were aggregated to the five MIX sectors and then assembled to monthly emission grid maps over Asia with a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ .", "Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (50% of total), NMVOC (38%),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (48%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (40%) emissions. Power plants have significant contributions for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (38% of total),  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  (29%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (34%) emissions.", "Table 3. National anthropogenic emissions in the MIX emission inventory in 2010 (units: Tg for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and Gg for other species). Bold values are the total emissions for Asian regions.", "Ratios of different species were widely used to inform emission characteristics. For example,  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  ratio was used as an indicator of coal combustion and emission control levels (Li et al., 2007), and ratios of  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  were used to inform combustion efficiency (Wang et al., 2010). Figure 7 compares regional emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$  estimated by the MIX inventory. Emission ratios of  $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$  are lowest in Other East Asia among different regions, which could be attributed to small share of coal use", "In this work, we also developed Asian emissions for 2006 and 2008 following the same approach of MIX, to illustrate the changes in Asian emissions from 2006 to 2010. Table 5 presents Asian emissions in 2006 and emission ratios of 2010 to 2016 by country. For the whole of Asia, emission growth rates from 2006 to 2010 are estimated as follows:  $-8.1\%$  for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $+19.2\%$  for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ ,  $+3.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}$ ,  $+15.5\%$  for NMVOC,  $+1.7\%$  for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $-3.4\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $-1.6\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $+5.5\%$  for BC,  $+1.8\%$  for OC, and  $+19.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Growth in  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions represent the continuously increasing energy use across Asia during 2006-2010, while different trends among species represents differences in the emission control level among sectors and regions. Compared to the increasing emission trends of all species during 2001-2006 (Zhang et al., 2009), the relatively flat or even decreasing emission trends in many species indicate the effectiveness of emission control measures in recent years (Gu et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013).", "MIX emission inventory can be accessed publicly from the website of http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. Both 2008 and 2010 emissions of 10 species with monthly variation at a spatial resolution of  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  are available from the website, including  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ , NMVOC,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, OC, and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Speciated NMVOC Emissions for CB05 and SAPRC-99 chemical mechanisms are provided at the same spatial and temporal resolution. The MIX in", "with REAS2 and EDGAR v4.2 (EC-JRC/PBL, 2011), two widely used inventories, to highlight the new findings from the mosaic inventory and identify the potential sources of uncertainties. We choose the year of 2008 to conduct the comparison because emissions after 2008 are not available in either REAS2 or EDGAR v4.2. Russian Asia was excluded from comparison. Asian anthropogenic emissions of MIX, REAS2, and EDGAR v4.2 in 2008 are tabulated in Table 7. Over Asia, MIX and REAS differ within  $10\%$  for most species, except for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  ( $18\%$  higher in REAS),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  ( $13\%$  higher), and BC ( $13\%$  lower). It is not surprising that the total Asian emission budgets in MIX and REAS2 are simi", "The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).", "When species in a specific inventory were missing, alternative estimates or datasets were used to fill the gap in which may involve additional uncertainties. In the MIX inventory,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions for Republic of Korea were roughly estimates from  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions in the CAPSS inventory and sector-specific emission ratios between  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011). For India, we used ANL-India for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC for all sectors and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  for power plants. REAS2 was used to fill the gap where emissions from ANL-India were absent (see Sect. 2.3 for detailed process procedure). For above cases where estimates of different species in the same country were obtained from", "In this work, we developed a new anthropogenic emission inventory for Asia for the years of 2008 and 2010 by constructing a mosaic of several regional and national emission inventories. MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. content.text: Compared with traditional techniques for forecasting air pollution, namely empirical approaches and simple statistical models, deep learning approaches can handle complex and non-linear relationships among air quality variables which allow them to better capture the pollution episode forecasts. Therefore, deep learning has been identified as a vital tool for air quality modelling and forecasting, which helps in recognizing pollution hot spots (Schmidhuber, 2015; Ong et al., 2016; Li et al., 2017).

In the future, more effort should be devoted to the representation of spatial proxies to improve spatial patterns in bottom-up emission inventories.

content.text: In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.

content.text: Because of the large spatial and temporal variability of biomass burning, emissions monitoring and forecasting must be based on satellite observations of the currently active fires (Kaiser et al., 2006). Several systems that monitor and forecast global and regional air quality and visibility include modules that calculate fire emissions from satellite-based observations of burnt area or hot spots (Freitas et al., 2005; Reid et al., 2009; Sofiev et al., 2009).

content.text: This paper describes the HTAP global air pollutant reference emission inventory for 2010, which is composed of latest available data from regional inventory compilers. It assures a consistent input for both regional and global modelling as required by the HTAP modelling exercise. The HTAP_v2.2 emission database makes use of consolidated estimates of official and latest available regional information with air pollutant grid maps from US EPA and Environment Canada for North America, EMEP-TNO for Europe, MIX for Asia, and the EDGARv4.3 database for the rest of the world. The mosaic of grid maps provides comprehensive local information on the emission of air pollutants, because it results from the collection of point sources and national emission grid maps at  $0.1^{\circ}$  (for some regions  $0.25^{\circ}$ ) resolution. Even though the HTAP_v2.2 data set is not a self-consistent bottom-up database with activity data of consistent international statistics, with harmonized emission factors, and with global sets of spatial proxy data, it provides a unique set of emission grid maps with global coverage and high spatial resolution, including in particular important point sources. The compilation of implied emission factors and per capita emissions for the different world regions using multiple sources provides the regional and national emission inventory compilers with a valuable asset for comparison with their own data for cross checking and analysis which may lead to identification of future improvement options.

content.text: obtained from the LandScan population database (ORNL, 2006). The Gridded Population of the World (GPW) population map is also frequently used to allocate emissions, but we do not include it in our analysis because the uncertainties introduced by differences in population maps are minor (Andres et al., 2016). In the second dataset (S2), all emissions are allocated based on the nighttime lights map drawn from the Defense Meteorological Satellite Program Operational Linescan System (DMSP-OSL) satellite (http://www.ngdc.noaa.gov/dmsp/download_rad_cal_96-97.html). These two scenarios, which involve a single type of spatial proxy for all sectors, are simplifications of the common practice utilized in current emission inventories. We use S1 as the base case and make modifications upon it to evaluate different spatial proxies used in different sectors. Figure 1 compares the spatial distributions of the total population and nighttime light data. The nighttime light data present more significant urban-rural gradients than the total population density data and, thus, may better represent differences in economic development levels between urban and rural areas.

Due to the shutdown of industrial activities and traffic volume, an obvious decline in primary air pollutants was observed in most Chinese cities in 2020 (Liu et al., 2020; Yuan et al., 2021). This will undoubtedly cause high uncertainty while investigating the effect of CCTPs on air quality. Therefore, the following data is only extracted from 2013 to 2019, and the AQI in 2020 is predicted through a deep learning network. Subsequently, the investigated data covers 8 years before and after the policy node, namely from 2013 to 2020, which is adequate for policy evaluation (Yu et al., 2021). In the following, air quality and meteorological data are obtained via a web crawler.

An emissions data set has been constructed using regional emission grid maps (annual and monthly) for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC and OC for the years 2008 and 2010, with the purpose of providing consistent information to global and regional scale modelling efforts.

orchestrator_plan.reason: This iterative query aims to detail methodologies combining emission inversion and chemical mechanisms for capturing and visualizing NMVOC emissions and HCHO data linked to HTAP_v2.2 and MIX inventories, addressing key integration needs implied by the track question.
orchestrator_plan.plansummary:
The retrieval task aimed to uncover effective methods for visualizing air pollution, with a specific focus on integrating advanced techniques for monitoring non-methane volatile organic compounds (NMVOCs) and formaldehyde (HCHO). This inquiry focused on leveraging emission inversion methods and detailed chemical mechanism assignments, with particular attention to data provided by the HTAP_v2.2 and MIX inventories. The iterative search employed a highly targeted semantic query to ensure precise retrieval of relevant materials.

### Search Execution

The semantic search strategy utilized a technically detailed query to capture the advanced techniques and datasets pertinent to the overarching objective. The query referenced specific terms such as "emission inversion methods," "chemical mechanism assignments," "NMVOC monitoring," and "HCHO visualization," thus ensuring relevance to both the atmospheric science and air pollution domains. The query was designed to find methodologies that synthesize these elements, reflecting the integration mindset implied by the user’s goal.

The system retrieved 20 results, showcasing a satisfactory hit rate for a focused, technical inquiry. Three example results maintained a relevance and credibility score of 8 out of 10, indicating a strong alignment with the query's intent and scientific rigor. Each of these results provided valuable insights into emission monitoring and visualization tools, although detailed evaluations of their utility or applicability were not explicitly included in the trace data.

### Retrieved Results and Key Findings

The selected results revealed insights into various methods and frameworks relevant to air pollution visualization:

1. **Emission Inversion and Chemical Mechanism Modeling**: One result emphasized utilizing emission inversion techniques to refine the accuracy of NMVOC emission estimates. Coupled with HCHO column retrievals, such approaches enable improved visualization of pollution dynamics at the regional and global scales. These insights suggest that advanced computational approaches, such as satellite data assimilation, are central to contemporary monitoring efforts.

2. **Chemical Mechanism Databases**: Another result focused on the development of chemical mechanism databases tailored to air quality modeling. Such resources are instrumental in defining the reactions and pathways critical for observing NMVOCs and HCHO. Including widely cited methodologies by William P.L. Carter (2015), this material appears particularly useful for crafting models with enhanced predictive accuracy.

3. **Integration of Satellite Data (TROPOMI)**: Additional findings highlighted the constraints of using satellite-derived HCHO observations (e.g., from TROPOMI) to monitor NMVOC emissions. These sources bridge observational data with emissions modeling, illustrating a clear route for visualization when using tools such as HTAP_v2.2 and MIX inventories. However, the implementation details were not fully covered within the trace.

The documents retrieved underscore that key advancements arise from hybrid methods combining empirical satellite data, sophisticated inversion algorithms, and chemical mechanisms. By focusing on datasets like HTAP_v2.2, which provides a globally harmonized anthropogenic emissions framework, and MIX, renowned for its integration of regional characteristics, researchers can achieve both fine-scale and broad-spectrum visualizations of air pollution metrics.

### Comments on Retrieval Quality

The retrieval quality demonstrates strong alignment with the user’s needs. The semantic query successfully targeted relevant, high-impact sources and brought forth techniques central to the desired visualization of NMVOC and HCHO data. Specific strengths of retrieval include:

- **Relevance**: The sourced material clearly engages with emission inversion, chemical mechanisms, and visualization, indicating that the crafted query successfully captured the core components of the user’s interest.
- **Credibility**: Scoring 8/10 for credibility, the results reflect robust scientific research or highly credible technical sources. The inclusion of established works, such as those by William P.L. Carter, bolsters the reliability of the findings.
- **Authority**: Many results are tied to significant research efforts in atmospheric science and appear to draw upon authoritative datasets and methodologies.
- **Completeness**: While the method-specific insights seem strong, the lack of explicit evaluation within this trace leaves some open questions regarding the completeness of implementation examples or practical workflows.

### Limitations and Suggestions for Improvement

Despite its strengths, there are some limitations in the retrieval process:
- **Detail in Evaluation**: Detailed insight reviews or comparisons were not documented in the trace. This gap slightly hampers the ability to discern how applicable or practical the retrieved methodologies might be in a real-world context.
- **Coverage of Visualization Techniques**: While the trace captures the modeling and data integration side of the problem, minimal emphasis is placed on the visualization tools or platforms themselves (e.g., GIS tools, graphical interfaces, etc.).
- **End-to-End Workflow Clarity**: The connection between modeling, retrieval, and user-end visualization could be more explicitly articulated to inform actionable steps.

### Conclusion

The semantic search process provided a strong foundation for understanding how to integrate emission modeling with chemical mechanisms to visualize NMVOC and HCHO distributions. Key datasets, such as HTAP_v2.2 and MIX, were effectively incorporated into the retrieval scope, offering pathways for advanced analysis and visualization methods. Despite the limitations in evaluation specificity and workflow completeness, the retrieved results reflect highly relevant, scientifically credible insights, well-suited for addressing the user’s query. For future iterations, incorporating visual-oriented tools or user-case documentation could further enrich the findings.
orchestrator_plan.hyde_hypothetical_paragraph_full_text: To enhance the monitoring of non-methane volatile organic compounds (NMVOCs) and formaldehyde (HCHO) visualization, this study integrates emission inversion techniques with chemical mechanism assignments, leveraging the HTAP_v2.2 and MIX emission datasets. Emission inversion methods, such as Bayesian optimization and adjoint modeling, are employed to refine spatial and temporal emission estimates, while chemical mechanisms like CB6 or MOZART are utilized to simulate NMVOC oxidation pathways and secondary HCHO formation. The HTAP_v2.2 and MIX inventories provide harmonized multi-regional emission inputs, enabling improved source attribution and intercontinental transport analysis. This integrated approach facilitates a more accurate representation of NMVOC emissions and their photochemical transformations, enhancing the reliability of HCHO column retrievals from satellite observations.

source_args.query_intent: techniques integrating emission inversion methods with chemical mechanism assignments for NMVOC monitoring and HCHO visualization using HTAP_v2.2 and MIX datasets

content.text: A top-down approach, utilizing observed data, has been developed for estimating VOCs emissions. For instance, based on aircraft and ground-based field measurements, the source-receptor relationships algorithm with Lagrangian particle dispersion model (Fang et al., 2016), mixed layer gradient techniques (Mo et al., 2020), eddy covariance flux measurements (Yuan et al., 2015), and box model (Wang et al., 2020) have been employed to complement or verify bottom-up results. However, these approaches do not comprehensively consider the complex nonlinear chemical reactions and transport processes that VOCs undergo in the atmosphere. Formaldehyde (HCHO) and glyoxal (CHOCHO) in the atmosphere serve as crucial oxidization intermediates for various VOCs (Hong et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2012). Satellite-based observations can readily detect their presence in the form of vertical column density (VCD) from space, making them widely utilized for estimating NMVOC emissions. A commonly used approach assumes that the observed HCHO/CHOCHO columns are locally linearly correlated with VOC emission rates (Palmer et al., 2006; Liu et al., 2012). However, this approach does not consider the spatial offset resulting from chemistry reactions and transport processes. Chaliyakunnel et al. (2019) conducted a Bayesian analysis to derive an optimal estimate of VOC emissions using HCHO measurements over the Indian subcontinent. Their results indicated that biogenic VOC emissions modeled by MEGANv2.1 were overestimated by approximately  $30 - 60\%$ , whereas anthropogenic VOC emissions derived from the RETRO inventory were underestimated by  $13 - 16\%$ . Cao et al. (2018) employed the GEOS-Chem model and its adjoint, incorporating tropospheric HCHO and CHOCHO column data from the GOME-2A and OMI satellites as constraints, to quantify Chinese NMVOC emissions. They demonstrated a low bias in the MEGAN model, in contrast to the significant overestimation shown in Bauwens et al. (2016), especially in southern China.

content.text: Non-methane volatile organic compounds (NMVOCs) are emitted into the atmosphere from surface anthropogenic, biogenic, and biomass burning sources. NMVOCs are precursors to tropospheric ozone and secondary organic aerosols, both of which are climate forcers and major air pollutants. NMVOCs also affect the oxidation capacity of the atmosphere, which in turn changes the lifetimes of greenhouse gases and other pollutants. It is thus crucial to quantify NMVOC emissions in order to understand their impacts on atmospheric chemistry and climate on both global and regional scales. Here we used satellite observations and a chemical transport model to constrain NMVOC emissions from China and assessed their impacts on seasonal surface ozone.

content.text: observations, respectively (Feng et al., 2022; Feng et al., 2020). The inversion process follows a two-step procedure within each inversion window, in which the emissions are inferred first and then input into the CMAQ model to simulate initial conditions of the next window. Meanwhile, the optimized emissions are transferred to the next window as prior emissions. -The two-step inversion strategy facilitates error propagation and iterative emission optimization, which have proven the superiority and robustness of our system in estimating emissions (Feng et al., 2023). In this study, we extended the data frame to include the assimilation of TROPOMI HCHO retrievals for optimizing NMVOC emissions. Concise descriptions of the forecast model, data assimilation approach, and experimental settings follow.

content.text: A complementary, "top-down" approach for quantifying emissions uses observations of the targeted species or its chemical derivatives, combined with a chemical transport model acting as a transfer function, to invert for the fluxes of the targeted species. In particular, tropospheric column concentrations of formaldehyde, retrieved from satellite UV-backscatter measurements, have been used to constrain NMVOC emissions. Formaldehyde is produced at high yields during the oxidation of many NMVOC species (Millet et al., 2006) and also emitted directly from anthropogenic and biomass burning activities (Akagi et al., 2011; Li et al., 2017). Early inversions of satellite-observed formaldehyde columns mostly focused on areas where the local NMVOC fluxes were dominated by biogenic sources during the growing season and in the absence of substantial biomass burning, such as the southeast US (Palmer et al., 2003, 2006; Milet et al., 2006, 2008), Europe (Dufour et al., 2009; Curci et al.,

content.text: We used the GEOS-Chem model and its adjoint, as well as satellite observations of tropospheric column concentrations of formaldehyde and glyoxal, to constrain monthly Chinese NMVOC emissions from anthropogenic, biogenic, and biomass burning sources for the year 2007. We updated the gas-phase chemistry in the GEOS-Chem model and constructed its adjoint. The a priori NMVOC emission estimates from biogenic, anthropogenic, and biomass burning sources were taken from the inventories developed by Guenther et al. (2006), Li et al. (2014, 2017), and Huang et al. (2012), respectively, as well as van der Werf et al. (2010). We con

content.text: State variables for emissions include  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  and NMVOCs. To reduce the degree of freedom in the analysis and avoid the difficulty associated with estimating spatiotemporal variations in background errors for individual species, we focus on optimizing the lumped total NMVOC emissions. During the forecast step, we differentiate individual NMVOC species emissions from the total NMVOC emissions using bottom-up statistical information. For a consistent comparison between simulations and observations, model-simulated  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  were diagnosed at the time and location of surface  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  measurements, whereas model-simulated HCHO was horizontally sampled to align with TROPOMI HCHO VCD retrievals, and subsequently integrated vertically.

content.text: We verified the adjoint model mathematically in two ways. Firstly, we used the adjoint model to calculate the sensitivities of global glyoxal and formaldehyde burdens to biogenic isoprene and anthropogenic xylene emissions, respectively, and found that the results reproduced the calculated sensitivities from the forward model (Fig. S1 in the Supplement). Secondly, we used a set of bottom-up NMVOC emission inventories (Sect. 2.2) to drive the forward model and took the resulting global tropospheric formaldehyde and glyoxal column concentrations as pseudo observations. We then used the pseudo observations of formaldehyde and glyoxal to successfully optimize back to the bottom-up NMVOC emission estimates over high-emission areas from an initial guess that was 5 times larger (Fig. S2). These experiments demonstrated the usefulness of the adjoint model for the inversion of NMVOC emissions.

content.text: We concluded that formaldehyde and glyoxal observations from GOME-2A and OMI provide quantitative constraints on the monthly emissions of Chinese NMVOCs. In particular, the simultaneous use of the observations of both species helped distinguish NMVOC precursors and thus provided better quantification of individual sources. However, better validation of these satellite data over China is urgently needed, particularly to resolve the apparent discrepancies among different retrievals for the same species. The monthly inversions presented in this work, conducted at  $5^{\circ}$  longitude  $\times 4^{\circ}$  latitude resolution due to limited computation resources, quantified the Chinese NMVOC emissions on regional and subregional scales. Future inversions and sensitivity studies targeting shorter periods of time may be conducted at finer resolutions to quantify Chinese NMVOC

content.text: Non-methane volatile organic compounds (NMVOC), serving as crucial precursors of  $\mathrm{O_3}$ , have a significant impact on atmospheric oxidative capacity and  $\mathrm{O_3}$  formation. However, both anthropogenic and biogenic NMVOC emissions remain subject to considerable uncertainty. Here, we extended the Regional multi-Air Pollutant Assimilation System (RAPAS) with the EnKF algorithm to optimize NMVOC emissions in China by assimilating TROPOMI HCHO retrievals. We also simultaneously optimize  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions by assimilating in-situ  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  observations to address the chemical feedback among VOC-  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ - $\mathrm{O_3}$ . Furthermore, a process-based analysis was employed to quantify the impact of NMVOC emission changes on various chemical reactions related to  $\mathrm{O_3}$  formation and depletion. NMVOC emissions exhibited a substantial reduction of  $50.2\%$ , especially in forest-rich areas of central and southern China, revealing a prior overestimation of biogenic NMVOC emissions. Compared with the forecast with prior NMVOC emissions, the forecast with posterior emissions The RAPAS significantly improved HCHO simulations, reducing biases by  $75.7\%$ , indicating a notable decrease in posterior emission uncertainties. Moreover, The forecast with posterior NMVOC emissions also effectively significantly corrected the overestimation of  $\mathrm{O_3}$  in forecast with prior emissions overestimation in  $\mathrm{O_3}$  simulations, reducing biases by  $49.3\%$ . This can be primarily attributed to a significant decrease in the  $\mathrm{RO}_2 + \mathrm{NO}$  reaction rate and an increase in the  $\mathrm{NO}_2 + \mathrm{OH}$  reaction rate in the afternoon, thus limiting  $\mathrm{O_3}$  generation. Sensitivity analyses emphasized the necessity of considering both NMVOC and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions for a comprehensive assessment of  $\mathrm{O_3}$  chemistry. This study enhances our understanding of the effects of NMVOC emissions on  $\mathrm{O_3}$  production and can contribute to the development of effective emission reduction policies.

content.text: In this study, we extended the RAPAS assimilation system with the EnKF assimilation algorithm to optimize NMVOC emissions using the TROPOMI HCHO retrievals. Taking the MEIC 2020 for anthropogenic emissions and MEGANv2.1 output for biogenic sources as a priori, NMVOC emissions over China in August 2022 were inferred. Importantly, we implicitly took the chemical feedback among VOC-  $\mathrm{NO}_x\mathrm{-O}_3$  into account by simultaneously adjusting  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions using nationwide in-situ  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  observations. Furthermore, we quantified the impact of NMVOC emission inversion on surface  $\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution using the CMAQ-IRR model.

args.query_intent: methods and computational frameworks for integrating spatial proxies, bottom-up emission inventories, and remote sensing satellite data into urban air pollution modeling and visualization systems

orchestrator_plan.reason: This query refines the effort to address the integration of various data sources like spatial proxies, emission inventories, and satellite data for improving urban air pollution visualization, focusing on multidisciplinary methods and frameworks.
orchestrator_plan.plansummary:
The root goal of this retrieval process was to explore how to effectively visualize air pollution, particularly by integrating diverse data sources such as spatial proxies, bottom-up emission inventories, and remote sensing satellite data into urban air pollution modeling and visualization systems. A single iteration was conducted using a semantic search strategy, tailored to locate multidisciplinary resources and computational frameworks relevant to the query.

### Strategy Details
The semantic search approach was employed, leveraging a refined query that emphasized integrating multi-source data for urban air pollution modeling and visualization. This included spatial proxies (e.g., spatially distributed measurements or indicators like population density), bottom-up emission inventories (e.g., localized emissions data based on ground reporting), and satellite remote sensing data (e.g., large-scale atmospheric pollutant measurements). The search aimed to aggregate diverse methods and computational frameworks applicable to urban environments. Twenty results were retrieved to match this query, representing potentially useful academic and technical content.

### Retrieval Outcomes
Three representative results were highlighted as relevant and credible:
1. **Result 1** and **Result 2**: These are likely identical or near-identical entries indicating academic research linked to air pollution modeling. Both were evaluated positively, receiving relevance and credibility scores of 8 (on an unspecified scale), suggesting strong alignment with the query intent but little detail was available from the summarization to further analyze the content.
   
2. **Result 3**: This described HTAP_v2.2, a mosaic of regional and global emission grid maps for 2008 and 2010 designed to study emissions and air quality. This dataset is particularly relevant for bottom-up emission inventories and modeling processes over extended spatial scales. Scored similarly in terms of relevance (8) and credibility (8), it aligns well with the aim of integrating spatial emissions data for urban air pollution studies.

### Observations on Key Insights
Detailed insight evaluation was skipped for all retrieved results, leaving a gap in understanding how well the retrieved documents inform key aspects of air pollution visualization, such as the modeling methodologies, specific computational techniques, or visualization tools discussed. As such, although the documents appear both relevant and credible based on their initial evaluations, their actual value for advancing the root goal remains unclear without a deeper analysis or content verification.

### Reflections on Retrieval Quality
- **Relevance**: The documents retrieved were positively rated for relevance, suggesting that the semantic query successfully captured the intended scope and topic of interest.
- **Credibility**: The results also received strong credibility scores, implying they likely originated from authoritative academic or technical sources, such as university research or international datasets.
- **Authority**: The included examples suggest reliance on established resources and academic authorship, further enhancing perceived authority.
- **Completeness**: The completeness of the retrieval process remains undetermined because key insight evaluation was omitted. As a result, it is unknown whether the retrieved materials comprehensively address the integration of spatial proxies, emission inventories, and satellite data into visualization systems.

### Summary of Analysis and Limitations
This retrieval round demonstrated potential for identifying relevant academic resources and datasets on the topic of urban air pollution modeling and visualization. However, the skipped evaluation steps for drawing insights from the retrieved materials hinder the ability to fully assess their applicability. In particular, a more thorough examination of:
- Specific frameworks or methods for integrating diverse data sources;
- Examples of visualization systems or tools applied in urban contexts; and
- The scalability and impact of applying remote sensing data alongside local emissions inventories,
is necessary to conclusively address the root goal. Further iterations refining the query or detailed content analysis may be required to close these gaps.
orchestrator_plan.hyde_hypothetical_paragraph_full_text: Integrating spatial proxies, bottom-up emission inventories, and remote sensing satellite data into urban air pollution modeling requires advanced computational frameworks that leverage geospatial data fusion techniques and machine learning algorithms. Spatial proxies, such as population density and land use patterns, can be combined with high-resolution bottom-up emission inventories to provide localized emission estimates, while satellite-derived aerosol optical depth (AOD) and trace gas concentrations offer broader spatial coverage and temporal variability. Hybrid modeling approaches, such as Bayesian hierarchical models or data assimilation techniques, enable the reconciliation of these heterogeneous data sources, improving the accuracy of pollutant concentration estimates. Visualization systems can utilize geographic information systems (GIS) and web-based platforms to render dynamic, high-resolution air quality maps, facilitating real-time monitoring and decision-making for urban air quality management.

Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.", "Figure 12.", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\mu \mathrm{m}$ ),  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. (2014) (see Fig. 3). Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).", "When species in a specific inventory were missing, alternative estimates or datasets were used to fill the gap in which may involve additional uncertainties. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ . source_args.query_intent: methods and computational frameworks for integrating spatial proxies, bottom-up emission inventories, and remote sensing satellite data into urban air pollution modeling and visualization systems

content.text: Based on our review of the spatial proxies used in bottom-up emission inventories for China, it can be concluded that such spatial proxies are empirical and may have introduced considerable uncertainties into the spatial distributions of emissions. Studies on fossil fuel  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  inventories have made tremendous efforts to improve the spatial distribution of emissions based on satellite-derived nighttime light (Oda et al., 2011), fuel sales and traffic data (McDonald et al., 2014), multivariate regressions (Wang et al., 2013; Gately et al., 2015), or combined fossil fuel data assimilation systems (Rayner et al., 2010; Asefi-Najafabady et al., 2014), shedding new light on ways to improve the spatial distribution of air pollutant inventories.

When further applying industrial gross domestic product (IGDP) values for the industrial sector, modeled  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns could better capture pollution hotspots in urban areas and exhibit the best performance of the six cases compared to satellite-based  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns (slope  $= 1.01$  and  $R^2 = 0.85$ ). This analysis provides a framework for information from satellite observations to inform bottom-up inventory development. In the future, more effort should be devoted to the representation of spatial proxies to improve spatial patterns in bottom-up emission inventories.

content.text: This paper describes the HTAP global air pollutant reference emission inventory for 2010, which is composed of latest available data from regional inventory compilers. It assures a consistent input for both regional and global modelling as required by the HTAP modelling exercise. The HTAP_v2.2 emission database makes use of consolidated estimates of official and latest available regional information with air pollutant grid maps from US EPA and Environment Canada for North America, EMEP-TNO for Europe, MIX for Asia, and the EDGARv4.3 database for the rest of the world. The mosaic of grid maps provides comprehensive local information on the emission of air pollutants, because it results from the collection of point sources and national emission grid maps at  $0.1^{\circ}$  (for some regions  $0.25^{\circ}$ ) resolution. Even though the HTAP_v2.2 data set is not a self-consistent bottom-up database with activity data of consistent international statistics, with harmonized emission factors, and with global sets of spatial proxy data, it provides a unique set of emission grid maps with global coverage and high spatial resolution, including in particular important point sources. The compilation of implied emission factors and per capita emissions for the different world regions using multiple sources provides the regional and national emission inventory compilers with a valuable asset for comparison with their own data for cross checking and analysis which may lead to identification of future improvement options.

source_args.query_intent: methods and computational frameworks for integrating spatial proxies, bottom-up emission inventories, and remote sensing satellite data into urban air pollution modeling and visualization systems

content.text: The spatial proxies used when developing gridded emission inventories are empirical and can introduce uncertainties in bottom-up emission inventories. This issue has rarely been evaluated. In this work, we evaluate the effects of spatial proxies on the representations of spatial distributions of emissions using an integrated framework of bottom-up emission inventories, a chemical transport model, and satellite observations. We first develop six sets of gridded  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions for China using the same magnitude of emissions from the MEIC and different spatial proxies. The spatial proxies considered in this study include the following: total population, nighttime lights, locations of power plants, IGDP, vehicle populations, and two different road network datasets. The nested-grid GEOS-Chem model is then used to simulate tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns using the six gridded emissions, and modeled  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns are compared to satellite-based  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns derived from OMI data.

content.text: In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.

content.text: Abstract. Spatial proxies used in bottom-up emission inventories to derive the spatial distributions of emissions are usually empirical and involve additional levels of uncertainty. Although uncertainties in current emission inventories have been discussed extensively, uncertainties resulting from improper spatial proxies have rarely been evaluated. In this work, we investigate the impact of spatial proxies on the representation of gridded emissions by comparing six gridded  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emission datasets over China developed from the same magnitude of emissions and different spatial proxies. GEOS-Chem-modeled tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns simulated from different gridded emission inventories are compared with satellite-based columns. The results show that differences between modeled and satellite-based  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns are sensitive to the spatial proxies used in the gridded emission inventories. The total population density is less suitable for allocating  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions than nighttime light data because population density tends to allocate more emissions to rural areas. Determining the exact locations of large emission sources could significantly strengthen the correlation between modeled and observed  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns. content.text: The results of this work emphasize the importance of spatial proxies for bottom-up emission inventory development. Discrepancies between models and observations should be attributed to not only errors in the magnitude of total emission estimates but also spatial proxies. Although the selection of spatial proxies in this work is still empirical and may not represent the best case, we illustrate methods for improving gridded emission inventories by carefully selecting spatial proxies. This study provides a framework to apply information from satellite observations to inform bottom-up inventory development. The approach used here could be further extended to other species and regions, and more advanced optimized approaches could be introduced into the development of emission inventories of different air pollu

content.text: obtained from the LandScan population database (ORNL, 2006). The Gridded Population of the World (GPW) population map is also frequently used to allocate emissions, but we do not include it in our analysis because the uncertainties introduced by differences in population maps are minor (Andres et al., 2016). In the second dataset (S2), all emissions are allocated based on the nighttime lights map drawn from the Defense Meteorological Satellite Program Operational Linescan System (DMSP-OSL) satellite (http://www.ngdc.noaa.gov/dmsp/download_rad_cal_96-97.html). These two scenarios, which involve a single type of spatial proxy for all sectors, are simplifications of the common practice utilized in current emission inventories. We use S1 as the base case and make modifications upon it to evaluate different spatial proxies used in different sectors. Figure 1 compares the spatial distributions of the total population and nighttime light data. The nighttime light data present more significant urban-rural gradients than the total population density data and, thus, may better represent differences in economic development levels between urban and rural areas.

content.text: The spatial distributions of tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns over China in the summer simulated from six gridded inventories are presented and compared with scenario-specific satellite-observed  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns in Fig. 4. Because all the inventories in model simulations have the same emission totals, differences among S1-S6 reflect differences in the spatial allocations of the total emissions. In general, modeled and observed  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns exhibit similar patterns but different fine structures. All modeled cases can reproduce highly polluted areas over the North China Plain and Yangtze River Delta, whereas many pollution hotspots in these regions are underestimated compared to the satellite data in S1. As discussed above, using the total population distribution as a spatial proxy may have misrepresented the urban-rural gradients of economic development levels and allocated disproportionately large fractions of emissions to rural regions. Consequently, urban emission hotspots may have been underestimated because in China the rural population exceeds the urban population.

Available emission inventories always differ in geographic region, time period, source classification, species, and spatial and temporal resolution, introducing complexities in intercomparisons of emissions and model results with different emission inputs. By involving the state-of-the-art local emission inventories developed with local knowledge and harmonizing them to uniform format, this approach can provide a reference on magnitude and spatial distribution of emissions for different regions, while there is always tradeoff in spatial-temporal coverage and resolution due to inconsistencies among involved inventories.

content.text: In this work, we use  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  as a case to study the influence of spatial proxies on spatial distributions of bottom-up emission inventories because  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  satellite retrieval is less uncertain and the spatial distributions of tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns are similar to those of surface  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions, especially in the summer when the lifetime of  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  is short (Richter et al., 2005; Beirle et al., 2003; Lin, 2012). Based on the same magnitude of  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions, we develop six sets of gridded emission data using different spatial proxies. We then use these gridded emissions and the nested-grid GEOS-Chem model to simulate tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns and compare them with satellite-based observations. The effects of spatial proxies on the modeled  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns and representations of different spatial proxies are evaluated and discussed.

args.query_intent: integration of satellite-based TROPOMI HCHO observations with emission inversion algorithms and chemical mechanism databases for NMVOC visualization on regional to global scales

orchestrator_plan.reason: This query complements previous searches by focusing specifically on TROPOMI satellite HCHO data integration with models and databases, essential for addressing visualization challenges for NMVOC emissions at multiple scales.
orchestrator_plan.plansummary:
The retrieval process aimed to address the key question: "How to visualize air pollution?" Specifically, it sought to support the integration of satellite-based TROPOMI (Tropospheric Monitoring Instrument) formaldehyde (HCHO) observational data with emission inversion algorithms and chemical mechanism databases to enable the visualization of non-methane volatile organic compound (NMVOC) emissions on both regional and global scales. This is a highly specialized query that intersects satellite data utilization, computational modeling, and scientific visualization, making its resolution critical for advanced air quality monitoring and research.

### Retrieval Strategy
A semantic search approach was implemented with the following query refinement: “integration of satellite-based TROPOMI HCHO observations with emission inversion algorithms and chemical mechanism databases for NMVOC visualization on regional to global scales.” This detailed and topic-specific phrasing was selected to pinpoint relevant academic, institutional, or methodological resources addressing the integration of these specific data and tools. The retrieval system was configured to request 20 results, aiming to balance specificity and breadth.

The query rationale emphasized the need for this particular focus, noting that previous exploratory attempts either left substantial gaps in terms of satellite-based HCHO integration or did not sufficiently address the visualization of NMVOC emissions across multiple scales. TROPOMI observations are particularly pertinent because of their high-resolution monitoring capabilities, which are crucial for understanding complex air pollution dynamics and producing actionable insights. Therefore, the search sought to uncover methodologies, tools, or case studies that highlight the interplay between satellite data, emission algorithms, and visualization techniques.

### Retrieval Outcomes
- **Hit Count**: The system retrieved 20 results. 
- **Evaluation Summary**: Three sample results were highlighted for evaluation. All three were kept for further consideration due to their high relevance and credibility scores (8/10 for each metric).
  
#### Sample Results:
1. **Result 1**: This article apparently addressed key elements such as NMVOC emissions, ozone ($\mathrm{O_3}$) pollution, HCHO column retrievals, and emission inversion methods. While the specific insights were not detailed in the trace, the relevance score of 8 suggests this document aligns well with the query’s focus areas.
2. **Result 2**: This source seemed connected with the National Center for Atmospheric Research (NCAR), potentially implicating high-quality data or modeling resources. It likely discussed atmospheric chemistry observations and modeling relevant to the use case.
3. **Result 3**: Authored by William P.L. Carter, this resource centered on the development of chemical mechanism databases—an essential component for integrating observational data with predictive models for air quality visualization.

While the trace does not provide granular insights extracted from these documents, the consistent high scores across both relevance (8/10) and credibility (8/10) suggest the retrieved results are authoritative and aligned with the task of integrating satellite data with modeling and visualization frameworks.

### Observations on Retrieval Quality
1. **Relevance**: The search strategy appears to have effectively captured documents that align closely with the aim of visualizing NMVOC emissions using TROPOMI satellite data, achieving a strong score for relevance in initial sample evaluations.
2. **Accuracy**: The semantic framing of the query helped ensure the retrieved documents emphasized satellite observational integration within modeling and database frameworks. Given the focus on scientific tools and methodologies, accuracy seems well-supported.
3. **Authority**: Sources include material from notable contributors in the field, such as specialized air quality research laboratories and recognized academics, bolstering the trustworthiness of the results.
4. **Completeness**: The search yielded a plausible breadth of 20 results, aiming for diversity in methodologies and applications. However, details within individual articles were not fully extracted or analyzed in this trace, leaving gaps in evaluating the overall comprehensiveness of retrieved data.

### Key Insights and Limitations
Although the evaluation phase within the trace was skipped, some recurring themes and contexts can be inferred from the result descriptions. The documents likely offered insights into:
- The use of TROPOMI data for HCHO column measurements, which are key indicators for understanding NMVOC distributions.
- Emission inversion techniques that leverage observational data to calculate source emissions—a fundamental step in modeling air quality data across various scales.
- The role of chemical mechanism databases in standardizing and interpreting observational and modeled data to produce consistent visualization outputs.

Despite this, the summarized results do not explicitly document experiments, tools, or workflows for air pollution visualization. Greater effort could be directed toward deeper content synthesis or iterative refinement based on these initial leads to fully map the integration of data, models, and visualization strategies.

### Conclusion
The first iteration of this retrieval process resulted in a strong start for uncovering components necessary for visualizing air pollution using satellite-based TROPOMI HCHO data. The high relevance and quality of the retrieved documents align with the technical and specific nature of the query. However, due to a lack of detailed evaluation or synthesis of the retrieved materials, actionable insights for immediate implementation remain incompletely articulated. Future iterations could refine the initial findings by extracting methodologies, tools, and visualization frameworks explicitly discussed in the retrieved data.
orchestrator_plan.hyde_hypothetical_paragraph_full_text: The integration of satellite-based TROPOMI formaldehyde (HCHO) observations with emission inversion algorithms and chemical mechanism databases enables high-resolution visualization of non-methane volatile organic compound (NMVOC) distributions on regional to global scales. TROPOMI's high spatial and temporal resolution provides robust constraints on HCHO columns, which serve as proxies for NMVOC oxidation. By coupling these observations with inverse modeling techniques, such as Bayesian or adjoint-based frameworks, and leveraging detailed chemical mechanisms like the Master Chemical Mechanism (MCM) or Carbon Bond Mechanism (CBM), it is possible to refine NMVOC emission estimates and assess their contributions to ozone and secondary organic aerosol formation. This integrated approach enhances our understanding of NMVOC sources, sinks, and their role in atmospheric chemistry, offering critical insights for air quality management and climate modeling.

content.text: Non-methane volatile organic compounds (NMVOCs) are emitted into the atmosphere from surface anthropogenic, biogenic, and biomass burning sources. NMVOCs are precursors to tropospheric ozone and secondary organic aerosols, both of which are climate forcers and major air pollutants. NMVOCs also affect the oxidation capacity of the atmosphere, which in turn changes the lifetimes of greenhouse gases and other pollutants. It is thus crucial to quantify NMVOC emissions in order to understand their impacts on atmospheric chemistry and climate on both global and regional scales. Here we used satellite observations and a chemical transport model to constrain NMVOC emissions from China and assessed their impacts on seasonal surface ozone.

content.text: A complementary, "top-down" approach for quantifying emissions uses observations of the targeted species or its chemical derivatives, combined with a chemical transport model acting as a transfer function, to invert for the fluxes of the targeted species. In particular, tropospheric column concentrations of formaldehyde, retrieved from satellite UV-backscatter measurements, have been used to constrain NMVOC emissions. Formaldehyde is produced at high yields during the oxidation of many NMVOC species (Millet et al., 2006) and also emitted directly from anthropogenic and biomass burning activities (Akagi et al., 2011; Li et al., 2017). Early inversions of satellite-observed formaldehyde columns mostly focused on areas where the local NMVOC fluxes were dominated by biogenic sources during the growing season and in the absence of substantial biomass burning, such as the southeast US (Palmer et al., 2003, 2006; Milet et al., 2006, 2008), Europe (Dufour et al., 2009; Curci et al.,

content.text: To investigate changes in the ozone  $(\mathrm{O}_3)$  chemical production regime over the contiguous United States (CONUS) with accurate knowledge of concentrations of its precursors, we applied an inverse modeling technique with Ozone Monitoring Instrument (OMI) tropospheric nitrogen dioxide  $(\mathrm{NO}_2)$  and total formaldehyde (HCHO) retrieval products in the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017, years in which United States National Emission Inventory were based. The inclusion of dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions and lightning-induced nitric oxide emissions significantly account for the contribution of background sources in the free troposphere. Satellite-constrained nitrogen oxide  $(\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}})$  and non-methane volatile organic compounds (NMVOCs) emissions mitigate the discrepancy between satellite and modeled columns: the inversion suggested 2.33-2.84 (1.07-1.34) times higher  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  over the CONUS (over urban regions) and 0.28-0.81 times fewer NMVOCs emissions over the southeastern United States. The model-derived HCHO/NO $_2$  column ratio shows gradual spatial changes in the  $\mathrm{O}_3$  production regime near urban cores relative to previously defined threshold values representing  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  and VOC sensitive conditions. We also found apparent shifts from the  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated regime to the transition regime (or the transition regime to the  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -limited regime) over the major cities in the western United States. In contrast, rural areas, especially in the east-southeastern United States, exhibit a decreased HCHO/NO $_2$  column ratio by  $-1.30 \pm 1.71$  with a reduction in HCHO column primarily driven by meteorology, becoming sensitive to VOC emissions. Results show that incorporating satellite observations into numerical modeling could help policymakers implement appropriate emission control policies for  $\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution.

content.text: A top-down approach, utilizing observed data, has been developed for estimating VOCs emissions. For instance, based on aircraft and ground-based field measurements, the source-receptor relationships algorithm with Lagrangian particle dispersion model (Fang et al., 2016), mixed layer gradient techniques (Mo et al., 2020), eddy covariance flux measurements (Yuan et al., 2015), and box model (Wang et al., 2020) have been employed to complement or verify bottom-up results. However, these approaches do not comprehensively consider the complex nonlinear chemical reactions and transport processes that VOCs undergo in the atmosphere. Formaldehyde (HCHO) and glyoxal (CHOCHO) in the atmosphere serve as crucial oxidization intermediates for various VOCs (Hong et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2012). Satellite-based observations can readily detect their presence in the form of vertical column density (VCD) from space, making them widely utilized for estimating NMVOC emissions. A commonly used approach assumes that the observed HCHO/CHOCHO columns are locally linearly correlated with VOC emission rates (Palmer et al., 2006; Liu et al., 2012). However, this approach does not consider the spatial offset resulting from chemistry reactions and transport processes. Chaliyakunnel et al. (2019) conducted a Bayesian analysis to derive an optimal estimate of VOC emissions using HCHO measurements over the Indian subcontinent. Their results indicated that biogenic VOC emissions modeled by MEGANv2.1 were overestimated by approximately  $30 - 60\%$ , whereas anthropogenic VOC emissions derived from the RETRO inventory were underestimated by  $13 - 16\%$ . Cao et al. (2018) employed the GEOS-Chem model and its adjoint, incorporating tropospheric HCHO and CHOCHO column data from the GOME-2A and OMI satellites as constraints, to quantify Chinese NMVOC emissions. They demonstrated a low bias in the MEGAN model, in contrast to the significant overestimation shown in Bauwens et al. (2016), especially in southern China.

content.text: Abstract. We used the GEOS-Chem model and its adjoint to quantify Chinese non-methane volatile organic compound (NMVOC) emissions for the year 2007, using the tropospheric column concentrations of formaldehyde and glyoxal observed by the Global Ozone Monitoring Experiment 2A (GOME-2A) instrument and the Ozone Monitoring Instrument (OMI) as quantitative constraints. We conducted a series of inversion experiments using different combinations of satellite observations to explore their impacts on the top-down emission estimates. Our top-down estimates for Chinese annual total NMVOC emissions were 30.7 to 49.5 (average 41.9)  $\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$ , including 16.4 to 23.6 (average 20.2)  $\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$  from anthropogenic sources, 12.2 to 22.8 (average 19.2)  $\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$  from biogenic sources, and 2.08 to 3.13 (average 2.48)  $\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$  from biomass burning. In comparison, the a priori estimate for Chinese annual total NMVOC emissions was  $38.3\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$ , including  $18.8\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$  from anthropogenic sources,  $17.3\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$  from biogenic sources, and  $2.27\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$  from biomass burning. The simultaneous use of glyoxal and formaldehyde observations helped distinguish the NMVOC species from different sources and was essential in constraining anthropogenic emissions. Our four in

content.text: We verified the adjoint model mathematically in two ways. Firstly, we used the adjoint model to calculate the sensitivities of global glyoxal and formaldehyde burdens to biogenic isoprene and anthropogenic xylene emissions, respectively, and found that the results reproduced the calculated sensitivities from the forward model (Fig. S1 in the Supplement). Secondly, we used a set of bottom-up NMVOC emission inventories (Sect. 2.2) to drive the forward model and took the resulting global tropospheric formaldehyde and glyoxal column concentrations as pseudo observations. We then used the pseudo observations of formaldehyde and glyoxal to successfully optimize back to the bottom-up NMVOC emission estimates over high-emission areas from an initial guess that was 5 times larger (Fig. S2). These experiments demonstrated the usefulness of the adjoint model for the inversion of NMVOC emissions.

content.text: More recently, satellite measurements of tropospheric glyoxal columns emerged as an additional constraint on NMVOC emissions (Stavrakou et al., 2009a). Like formaldehyde, glyoxal is produced during the oxidation of many NMVOCs (including most importantly isoprene), as well as emitted directly from biomass burning (Fu et al., 2008; Myriokefalitakis et al., 2008). In particular, glyoxal is produced at high yields at the initial ring-cleaving stage during the oxidation of aromatics (Volkamer, 2001; Nishino et al., 2010), which are mainly anthropogenic. In contrast, the production of formaldehyde from the oxidation of aromatics is further downstream and thus spatially diffuse (Volkamer, 2001). As such, simultaneous analyses of formaldehyde and glyoxal observations can help differentiate between biogenic and anthropogenic NMVOC emissions. Stavrakou et al. (2009a) pioneered a two-compound inversion using tropospheric glyoxal and formaldehyde column observations from the Scanning Imaging Absorption spectrometer for Atmospheric CHartographY (SCIAMACHY) satellite instrument to constrain the global sources of glyoxal. They estimated that the anthropogenic NMVOC fluxes over East Asia for the year 2005 were a factor of 2 to 3 larger than the bottom-up estimates of the Emission Database for Global Atmospheric Research (EDGAR, v3.3) inventory (Olivier et al., 2001, 2002) and the REanalysis TROpospheric (RETRO) emission inventory (Schultz et al., 2007). In addition, they inferred a large missing source of glyoxal over the global continents, which they attributed to production from an unknown biogenic precursor.

content.text: We concluded that formaldehyde and glyoxal observations from GOME-2A and OMI provide quantitative constraints on the monthly emissions of Chinese NMVOCs. In particular, the simultaneous use of the observations of both species helped distinguish NMVOC precursors and thus provided better quantification of individual sources. However, better validation of these satellite data over China is urgently needed, particularly to resolve the apparent discrepancies among different retrievals for the same species. The monthly inversions presented in this work, conducted at  $5^{\circ}$  longitude  $\times 4^{\circ}$  latitude resolution due to limited computation resources, quantified the Chinese NMVOC emissions on regional and subregional scales. Future inversions and sensitivity studies targeting shorter periods of time may be conducted at finer resolutions to quantify Chinese NMVOC
